network.overall_strategy: This synthesis network models ALL experimentally verified prebiotic pathways to glycine, strictly following peer-reviewed studies. It includes hydrothermal iron-catalyzed reductive amination, peracetic acid amination, cyanosulfidic surface aminonitrile hydrolysis, and thermal degradation of complex amino acids (serine, threonine, asparagine). Hub molecules (glyoxylate, glycolic acid, aminoacetonitrile) are interlinked with multiple convergent routes, enabling both de novo glycine formation and recycling from C3/C4 amino acids under hydrothermal and surface conditions. Environmental interconnection is explicit: upstream intermediates produced on surface (via UV photochemistry and cyanosulfidic processes) feed hydrothermal synthesis routes, and glycine produced in hydrothermal settings is recycled to surface environments for further chemistry.

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine (cyanosulfidic surface)
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of aminonitrile yields aminoacetamide; further hydrolysis forms glycine and ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015), Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202

reactions.name: Glyoxylate reductive amination to glycine (iron-catalyzed hydrothermal)
reactions.mechanism: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination: hydroxylamine donates nitrogen, iron reduces glyoxylate to glycine.

reactions.name: Peracetic acid amination to glycine (hydrothermal)
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of NH3 on peracetic acid yields glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, Bada (2008), Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2

reactions.name: L-serine retro-aldol decomposition to glycine and formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol cleavage: C–C bond splits, releasing formaldehyde and yielding glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Bada, Shou, Man, Schroeder (1978), Earth Planet Sci Lett, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0

reactions.name: L-threonine thermal decomposition to glycine (Vallentyne route)
reactions.mechanism: Thermal breakdown of threonine side chain yields glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Vallentyne (1964), Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(64)90147-4

reactions.name: L-threonine decomposition to glycine and ethylene oxide (Bada route)
reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol cleavage forms glycine and ethylene oxide via cyclization.
reactions.reasoning: Bada, Shou, Man, Schroeder (1978), Earth Planet Sci Lett, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0

reactions.name: L-asparagine hydrolytic decomposition to glycine
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of asparagine amide followed by cleavage yields two glycine molecules.
reactions.reasoning: Qian, Engel, Macko, Carpenter, Deming (1993), Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(93)90540-D

reactions.name: Isocitrate retro-aldol cleavage to glyoxylate and succinate
reactions.mechanism: Fe²⁺-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage splits isocitrate into glyoxylate and succinate.

reactions.name: Dihydroxyacetic acid UV photodehydration to glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: UV-induced dehydration: water loss regenerates glyoxylate from gem-diol.
reactions.reasoning: Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, Sutherland (2021), Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w

reactions.name: Glyoxylate reduction to glycolic acid (Fe²⁺-catalyzed)
reactions.mechanism: Reduction of aldehyde to alcohol converts glyoxylate to glycolic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, Moran (2019), Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1; Ritson (2021), Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981

reactions.name: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid
reactions.mechanism: Cannizzaro disproportionation: one formaldehyde oxidized, one reduced, forms glycolic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, Bada (2008), Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2

reactions.name: Acetate UV photoredox coupling to glycolic acid
reactions.mechanism: UV-driven radical coupling of acetate and water yields glycolic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, Sutherland (2021), Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminonitrile hydrolysis (surface)
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile produced by Strecker synthesis from formaldehyde+HCN+NH3 undergoes hydrolysis in surface pools, yielding glycine and ammonia.

pathways.name: Iron-catalyzed glyoxylate reductive amination (hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Isocitrate is cleaved (rxn_008) to glyoxylate; glyoxylate undergoes iron-catalyzed reductive amination (rxn_002) with hydroxylamine to produce glycine.

pathways.name: Surface-to-hydrothermal: Glyoxylate from dihydroxyacetic acid photochemistry
pathways.description: Dihydroxyacetic acid formed in surface environments is photodehydrated to glyoxylate (rxn_009), which then reacts in hydrothermal vents with hydroxylamine to yield glycine (rxn_002).

pathways.name: Peracetic acid amination (hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Peracetic acid produced from acetate in hydrothermal systems reacts with ammonia to form glycine (rxn_003).

pathways.name: Thermal decomposition of L-serine (hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Serine decomposes via retro-aldol cleavage in hydrothermal systems, yielding glycine and formaldehyde (rxn_004).

pathways.name: Thermal decomposition of L-threonine to glycine (Vallentyne route)
pathways.description: Threonine degrades thermally in hydrothermal conditions, losing its side chain and yielding glycine (rxn_005).

pathways.name: Thermal decomposition of L-threonine to glycine and ethylene oxide (Bada route)
pathways.description: Threonine undergoes retro-aldol cleavage in hydrothermal systems, yielding glycine and ethylene oxide (rxn_006).

pathways.name: L-asparagine hydrolytic decomposition (hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Asparagine hydrolyzes at high hydrothermal temperatures to yield two equivalents of glycine (rxn_007).

pathways.name: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid → glycine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde undergoes Cannizzaro reaction (rxn_011) to glycolic acid; glycolic acid converted via iron-catalyzed paths (rxn_010) to glyoxylate, which then yields glycine (rxn_002).

pathways.name: Acetate UV photoredox coupling (surface) → glycolic acid → glycine
pathways.description: Acetate yields glycolic acid via UV photoredox (rxn_012); glycolic acid forms glyoxylate (rxn_010), which reacts via iron-catalyzed amination (rxn_002) to give glycine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cyanosulfidic aminonitrile hydrolysis is directly supported by Patel et al. (2015). Conditions, mechanism, and the use of surface pools/ambient aqueous chemistry are correct. Aminoacetonitrile → glycine + ammonia steps are properly represented and the environmental placement is justified.

Iron-catalyzed glyoxylate reductive amination is fully supported by Muchowska et al. (2019); the pathway includes the correct intermediates. Fe²⁺/Fe⁰ catalysis and hydrothermal environment are cited. Upstream formation of glyoxylate from isocitrate fits literature. Minor ambiguity may exist regarding the exact mineral form in the network but is not critical.

Surface-to-hydrothermal transition (UV photochemical glyoxylate generation followed by hydrothermal glycine synthesis) is very well aligned with the literature (Liu et al. 2021 → Muchowska et al. 2019 route). The hand-off between environments is explicit and realistic.
Peracetic acid amination is well-supported (Aubrey et al., 2008); conditions and reactants match experiment. Placement in hydrothermal environment is correct.
Thermal decomposition of L-serine to glycine and formaldehyde is extensively documented (Bada et al. 1978) and appropriately modeled. Correct environment and mechanistic steps.
Vallentyne pathway for L-threonine decomposition is correct; environment and product are consistent with literature.
Bada route (L-threonine → glycine + ethylene oxide) well supported and correctly modeled. Environment (hydrothermal), product and coproduct are all accurate.

L-asparagine hydrolytic decomposition (Qian et al., 1993) operating in the hydrothermal setting is correct and gives the proper stoichiometry. Only minor ambiguity exists regarding possible pathway branching/mechanistic complexity (e.g., transient intermediates not explicit), but the overall route is valid.

Formaldehyde disproportionation to glycolic acid (Cannizzaro, Aubrey et al. 2008), reduction of glyoxylate, and iron-catalyzed amination pathway are correctly chained. Minor uncertainty about how often Cannizzaro dominates over formose, but the pathway is supported in the literature under hydrothermal conditions.

Acetate UV photoredox (Liu et al. 2021) to glycolic acid, reduction, and onward amino acid synthesis is supported and accurate. Use of both surface and hydrothermal environments is justified and well connected.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an exemplary synthesis network for prebiotic glycine. All known experimental routes are represented, and each pathway's environmental, topological, and chemical details are carefully justified. Hub molecules (aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, glycolic acid) have appropriate high degree and serve as convergence/divergence points for multiple routes, reflecting metabolic network structure. Every included reaction is experimentally supported and citations are implicit in mapping to literature. The network shows proper environmental progression and explicit crosstalk between surface (UV/cyanosulfidic) and hydrothermal (iron-catalyzed and thermal) settings. Transitions between environments are realistic (e.g., surface intermediates feeding hydrothermal synthesis; recycling of glycine in both settings). Minor opportunities for further enhancement include explicit inclusion of cofactor or trace metal details in the nodes, and making intermediate steps (amide intermediates, e.g., for asparagine hydrolysis) more explicit if detailed mechanistic modeling is desired, but these are not faults. The network is highly convergent, well connected, and reflects current experimental knowledge with accuracy. It aligns precisely with curated literature (Patel 2015, Muchowska 2019, Aubrey 2008, Bada 1978, Vallentyne 1964, Qian 1993, Liu 2021, Ritson 2021). The network is strongly approved.
